coredata.dc:title: Asymmetric and zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitors based on iron oxide and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yetiman, Sevda
Kılıç Dokan, Fatma
Onses, M. Serdar
Huang, Xian
Yılmaz, Erkan
Sahmetlioglu, Ertugrul
coredata.dc:description: There is an urgent need to design and develop materials for high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems. This paper presents practical synthesis and novel combination of α-Fe2O3, carbon dots (CDs), and BiOCl for high performance asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) and zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitors (ZHSCs). The materials prepared by wet-chemical methods were probed with different analytical techniques. X-Ray Diffraction, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy) and Raman analyzes indicate presence of alkyl and aryl rich surface functional groups of CDs which contribute to conducive interface for the interplay between electrolyte and electrodes. The α-Fe2O3@CDs enabled ~20 fold increase in the electrochemical performance; 3686 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g −1 in 2 M KOH electrolyte. However, the maximum specific capacitance of pristine α-Fe2O3 was just 184 F g−1 at the same current density. ASCs and ZHSCs were then fabricated using α-Fe2O3@CDs as the cathode along with anodes composed of BiOCl and Zn foil, respectively. The assembled ASC device achieved the potential window of 1.8 V with a high specific capacitance of 251 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The highest energy, and power densities were 28 Wh kg−1 and 14.8 kW kg−1, respectively. The formed ZHSC exhibited the maximum specific capacitance of 449 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with 78 % retention after 15,000 cycles. ZHSC outperformed ASC in terms of the energy and power density by ~5 (140 Wh kg−1) and ~ 2 (30 kW kg−1) times, respectively. These results position α-Fe2O3@CDs as an extremely promising material for ASCs and ZHSCs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an urgent need to design and develop materials for high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems. This paper presents practical synthesis and novel combination of α-Fe2O3, carbon dots (CDs), and BiOCl for high performance asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) and zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitors (ZHSCs). The materials prepared by wet-chemical methods were probed with different analytical techniques. X-Ray Diffraction, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy) and Raman analyzes indicate presence of alkyl and aryl rich surface functional groups of CDs which contribute to conducive interface for the interplay between electrolyte and electrodes. The α-Fe2O3@CDs enabled ~20 fold increase in the electrochemical performance; 3686 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g −1 in 2 M KOH electrolyte. However, the maximum specific capacitance of pristine α-Fe2O3 was just 184 F g−1 at the same current density. ASCs and ZHSCs were then fabricated using α-Fe2O3@CDs as the cathode along with anodes composed of BiOCl and Zn foil, respectively. The assembled ASC device achieved the potential window of 1.8 V with a high specific capacitance of 251 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The highest energy, and power densities were 28 Wh kg−1 and 14.8 kW kg−1, respectively. The formed ZHSC exhibited the maximum specific capacitance of 449 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with 78 % retention after 15,000 cycles. ZHSC outperformed ASC in terms of the energy and power density by ~5 (140 Wh kg−1) and ~ 2 (30 kW kg−1) times, respectively. These results position α-Fe2O3@CDs as an extremely promising material for ASCs and ZHSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for sustainable energy-storage systems have become critically momentous with the rapid industrialization and digitation [1–10]. Supercapacitors (SCs) are among the most inevitable electrochemical energy-storage devices for future regenerative energy systems owing to having almost limitless lifespan, charge-discharge capability, and outstanding power density [11–15]. Nevertheless, the low energy density of SCs, compared to batteries, is the biggest obstacle restricting their applications. Pseudocapacitors can enhance the specific capacity (Sc) by battery-like redox reactions; however, water decomposition exhibits a limit over the utilized voltage window of aqueous electrolyte. Consequently, boosting the applied voltage window of aqueous SCs is pivotal for the design and development of high-efficiency SCs [15,16].

In principal, pristine metal electrodes are eligible to satisfy long cycle stability with high energy and power densities based on the high transfer of electrons through the charge-discharge period and eminently reversible redox reactions [17–20]. Zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitors (ZHSCs) are the energy storage systems recently developed based on this principle. Mainly, ZHSCs comprise a battery-kind zinc foil anode in which the electrochemical energy storage occurs by the electroplating/peeling of zinc foil anode (caused by oxidation/reduction of Zn2+, Zn ⇔ Zn2++2e−) and the adsorption/desorption of cathode in an electrolyte that involves Zn salts suchlike ZnCl2, ZnSO4 or Zn [CF3SO3]2 ions dissolved in water, acidic or rarely organic solution [21–29].

Particularly in recent years, there has been an increase in reports to design and optimize nanostructured electrode materials for SCs. Nanomaterials are typically classified as zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), and two-dimensional (2D). 0D nanostructures are often defined as spherical particles with three nanoscale-restricted dimensions (typically 1–100 nm). Depending on the length/diameter ratio, 1D nanostructures are generally classified as nanorods or nanotubes. 2D materials are typically ones with a few atomic layers of thickness and two additional dimensions beside the nanometric dimension [11,30–35] Especially, after the discovery of graphene (a one-atom-thick 2D mono layer composed of sp2-hybrid carbon) nano carbon materials also arouse highly interest for ES applications.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs); single-walled (SWCNTs) or multi-walled (MWCNTs) are one of the well-known 1D carbon materials extensively used in SCs [36–38] Yet another 1D carbon materials are carbon nanofibers (CNFs) which can be obtained by electrospinning methods or chemical vapor deposition [13]. Many fibrous biomasses, such as cotton, ramie, and wood have been widely used as precursors to obtain 1D carbon electrodes. 0D carbon materials such as carbon nanosphere, activated carbon (AC), and, mesoporous carbon are sphere-shaped carbon particles [34,39–41]. Large specific surface areas (hundreds to thousands of m2/g) with tunable pore size and distribution consecrates 0D carbon materials for ES application. The most trendy 0D carbonaceous materials are carbon dots (CDs), with a size between 0 and 50 nm, which possess marvelous features such as satisfactory chemical stability, facile synthesis from economical materials, ability to accommodate ions [3,42,43]. Moreover, hydrophilic surface functional groups (-OH, -OOH, and -COOR) of CDs facilitate the interplay between electrodes and electrolytes.

Recently, bismuth-based materials have been considered as an alternative anode material to carbon since Wang et al. showed that the specific capacitance of BiPO4 (360.56 F g−1) was almost 3.1 times higher than activated carbon (117.3 F g−1) at a current density of 1 Ag−1, with an enhanced potential window of −1.2-0.2 V [44]. Among various bismuth-based materials, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl), has a tetragonal lamellar structure in which (Cl-Bi-O-Bi-Cl) sheets are stacked together by the van der Waals interplay through the Cl atoms along the c-axis. BiOC is a common choice due to its wide potential window, outstanding electrochemical durability, excellent oxide ion conductivity, wide band gap (~3.3 eV), cost-effective yet nontoxic nature, and ease of access to different morphologies [45–49].

There have been numerous studies focused on investigating pristine CDs and BiOCl or their synergistic effect with different materials such as metal oxides, sulfides, or conductive polymers for electrochemical energy storage applications. For instance, Naushad et al. reported the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and obtained the maximum specific capacitance of 1782 F g −1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1; 1867 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 [50]. Xia et al. investigated the electrochemical features of BiOCl @MXene composite material formed with an easy, low-cost chemical bath deposition method. The obtained highest volumetric specific capacitance was 396.5 F cm3 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Moreover, the symmetric SC (SSC) achieved a high power density of 567.4 W kg−1 at a high energy density of 15.2 Wh kg−1 with 85 % retention after 5000 cycles [51].

Herein, we propose unique hybridization of α-Fe2O3 and CDs for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) and ZHSCs. α-Fe2O3 and carbon dots were prepared via the hydrothermal route, which is an easy and eco-friendly method to form nanocomposites. α-Fe2O3 was chosen as the essence metal oxide due to its low-cost, high abundance, facile synthesis, and good chemical stability in alkaline electrolytes. At first, the electrochemical performance was probed by using two different kinds of aqueous electrolytes: KOH and NaOH. Afterward, α-Fe2O3 was combined with CDs. The results show that the composite α-Fe2O3@CDs yielded a ~ 20 fold increase in the SC performance compared to pristine α-Fe2O3. Thereafter, the best-yielded α-Fe2O3@CDs electrode was utilized as the cathode material for ASCs and ZHSCs using anodes composed of BiOCl and Zn foil, respectively. The electrochemical performance of SC devices was studied in detail. To the best of our knowledge, this study has one of the highest specific capacitances in a three electrode-system among α-Fe2O3 based SCs. Furthermore, this report is one of the rare studies of BiOCl used as an anode in ASC. Moreover, this is the first study to investigate the ZHSC properties of an α-Fe2O3 based composite.

Iron (III) chloride (FeCl3, 99.0 %), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥ 99.0 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥ 85.0 %), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7.H2O, 99.5 %), glycine(C₂H₅NO₂), n-Butanol (C₄H₁₀O, ≥99.5 %), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, ≥ 99.9 %), acetic acid (CH₃COOH, 99.8 %), and potassium chloride (KCl, ≥ 99.5 %), and ethanol (CH3CH2OH, >99 %)were obtained from Merck (GERMANY). Sigma-Aldrich (USA) was the supplier company for polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 99.9 %) and n-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.0 %). All chemical reagents and solvents for synthesis and analysis were utilized without further purification and the used deionized water (DI) was 18.2 MΩ cm resistivity.

The synthesis of α-Fe2O3 was done with small modifications of the previously reported procedure [52] . In detail, 3 mmol FeCl3 was dissolved in 35 mL DI until a homogenous solution was observed. In another beaker 1.6 mmol, Na2SO4 was dissolved in 20 mL DI with ultrasonication for 20 min. Thereafter, these two different solutions were mixed and ultrasonicated for another 1 h. Subsequently, the obtained dark brown homogenous solution was hydrothermally treated for 6 h at 120 °C. After the hydrothermal chamber reached to room temperature, the brownish dispersion was centrifuged after washing with DI and ethanol. As-obtained dark brown particles were vacuum dried at 90 °C for 15 h and calcinated at 450 °C for 2.5 h at a rate of 1 °C min−1, in flowing air to obtain the pure α -Fe2O3.

A simple hydrothermal process was used to synthesize CDs. Typically, 1.0 g of the citric acid monohydrate and 0.5 g of glycine were dispersed in 40 mL DI solution and ultrasonicated until the powders were completely dissolved. The obtained homogenous solution was hydrothermally treated for 5 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, blue emission CDs solution was obtained. To remove the impurities, the solution was diffused with a 2:1 volume ratio of n-butanol and evaporated for 2 h [42]. The obtained goopy material was solid CDs with blue emission (Fig. S1†).

The α-Fe2O3@CDs compound was produced as follows. Firstly, 10 mL of CDs solution was prepared by dissolving 2 mg/mL CDs into DI with ultrasonic irradiation for 10 min. Then, 200 mg of pure α-Fe2O3 was mixed with that solution, aged for 2 h, centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and vacuum dried at 85 °C for 5 h.

The synthesis procedure of BiOCl was based on a recent report by Zheng et al. [53]. Typically, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3∙5H2O were mixed with 50 mL of concentrated acetic acid solution (H2O/ acetic acid: 50 mL/2.5 mL.) Meanwhile, 2 mmol of KCl solution (15 mL) was prepared in the other beaker. Thereafter, these two solutions were mixed by ultrasonication and aged for 24 h. The obtained white precipitate was centrifuged several times by washing with DI and ethanol, latterly vacuum dried at 85 °C for 15 h. The schematic illustration of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 .

The crystallographic structure of the materials was analyzed with Bruker AXS D8 X-ray powder diffractometer with simple cubic lattice and CU Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm), (the scan range (2θ) changing 5° to 90°). To screen the chemical bonds of the molecules Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Spectrum Two, Perkin-Elmer, USA) was carried out. Raman spectra of the samples were scrutinized by WITec alpha 300 M dispersive Raman spectrometer consisting of HeNe laser system (Excitation of wavelength is 532 nm). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), and (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) BJH methods were operated to obtain the pore size distribution, total pore volume (TPV), and the specific surface area (SSA) of the materials. The morphology of the patterns was detected by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM, Gemini 550) and Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope (STEM, Gemini 550).

To form the working electrodes, the electroactive materials (α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3@CDs, and BiOCl): 85 wt%, acetylene black: 10 wt%, and PDVF:5 wt% were mingled with the involvement of a few drops of NMP solvent. The elective current collector was (1 cm × 1 cm) nickel foam which was used after cleaning with 1 M HCl to remove the oxide layer on it. The electrode materials were produced by dropping the as-prepared electroactive materials' slurry on the nickel foam and vacuum drying at 100 °C for 15 h. Approximately 1.5 mg mass of the active materials were obtained.

All of the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were implemented by Gamry Referance,3000 Electrochemical Workstation. The half-cell analyses were operated in a typical three-electrode system in 2 M KOH, and 2 M NaOH aqueous electrolytes by using the prepared materials as working electrodes, silver /silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) as the reference electrode, and platinum (Pt) foil (1 cm × 1 cm) as the counter electrode.

The ASC device was formed by using BiOCl as anode and α-Fe2O3@CDs as cathode with a glass microfiber (Whatman) separator at the voltage range of 0 to 1.8 V. ZHSC was assembled by using α-Fe2O3@CDs as cathode, Zn foil as both the anode and current collector in 2 M KOH + 0.05 M ZnSO₄ electrolyte, with a glassy fiber filter paper separator at the potential range of 0.7–2.2 V. Eqs. (4), (5), and (6) were utilized to reckon the specific capacitance (Csp, F g−1), the energy density (E, Wh kg−1), and the power density (P, W kg−1) of the ZHSC device without multiplication or dividing four (4) factor.

To scrutinize the structure and phase character of the samples, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 2a) was implemented. The peaks detected at 25.50°(012), 34.56°(104), 37.04°(110), 41.61°(113), 49.91° (024), 55. 24°(116), 58.14°(018), 65.2°(214) and,68.4° (300) are the characteristic of α-Fe2O3 intensively signaled, which is attributed to as the hexagonal structure of hematite (α-Fe2O3, JPCDS card no. 33–0664) [54–60]. For the composite α-Fe2O3@CDs material, the peak observed at 19.5° belongs to the (022) plane of CDs confirming the presence of CDs. For the BiOCl, the peaks at around 11.9°(001), 4.04°(011), 25.91°(110), 32.61°(012), 33.42°(003), 36.34°(112), 40.90°(020), 46.76°(113), 48,44°(121), 54.22°(122), 58.66°(220), 68.22°(214),74.91°(311) are compatible with the standard card peaks (JCPDS 06–0249) [61].

To better explain the phase structure of the patterns the Rietveld XRD refinement was performed by the means of High Score Plus Software. Table 1 and Fig. S2† present Rietveld analysis parameters, and Rietveld fitted X-ray diffraction of the samples. The conformable fitting indicates the synthesis of pristine materials was performed successfully. The axis ratio c/a of α-Fe2O3 is generally shrunk along the c-axis. Thus, the acquired value of 2.72 is close to its theoretical value of 2.83 (Fig. S2†. a) [62]. Although the hexagonal crystal structure of pristine α-Fe2O3 did not change with the addition of CDs, there was evident alteration in the lattice parameter which deduced the axis ratio c/a down to 2.27 (Fig. S2†.b) . Additionally, the calculated lattice parameters of BiOCl are in good agreement with the literature, where a = b = 3.88 Å,and c = 7.41 Å (Fig. S2†.c) [63,64].

The average crystalline sizes of the particles were calculated by two different methods from XRD results; Debye Scherer model (DS) and Williamson and Hall model (WH), respectively. Gaussian model was used to fit the data.

All peaks of the patterns were assessed for the measurement [65,66].

In Table S1† crystalline sizes analysis data for the DS model is shown. The calculated average crystalline sizes of α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3@CDs, and BiOCl are 26.78 nm, 7.50 nm, and 29.65 nm, respectively. The average crystalline size of α-Fe2O3 was significantly lessened with the addition of CDs.

The average crystalline size of α-Fe2O3 was 2.32 nm and the powder has 39 × 10−3 strain. With the addition of CDs, the average crystalline size of the composite α-Fe2O3@CDs decreased down to 1.2 nm with a 5.62 × 10−3 strain. The average crystalline size and the strain of BiOCl were 75.0 nm, and 5.09 × 10−3, respectively. To avoid the negative values of the slope and intercept some of the data were masked (Fig. S3† red dots). This requirement might cause discrepancy in results obtained from DS and WH models. Additionally, the evaluation of low-intensity peaks might also have a negative effect on this situation.

FTIR analysis (Fig. 2b) was implemented to scrutinize the chemical formation and chemical bonding of the samples. In the case of α-Fe2O3, the extensive band at 3450 cm−1 is ascribed, to the OH stretching vibration. The peaks observed at around 1640 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 belong to symmetric and asymmetric bending vibration of CO. The peak at 895 cm −1 is caused by CH stretching vibration, and FeO stretching corresponds to the peaks at 480 and 562 cm−1 [70]. The FTIR analysis of α-Fe2O3@CDs shows the existence of the carboxylic group, which was validated by specific absorption bands of OH at 3430 cm −1, and the stretching vibration bands of CO, CO at 1130 cm−1, 1710 cm −1, respectively. Moreover, the intense peak at 1572 cm−1 refers to the vibration and deformity bands of N − H, when the peak around 2300 cm −1 assigned to be -C ≡ N signifies the presence of amino-inclusive functional groups. Furthermore, the adsorption peaks depicted at 2937 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 682 cm−1 refer to the stretching and bending vibrations of C − H, CC and = C-H respectively, confirming the existence of alkyl and aryl groups [71–73]. Additionally, the structure has all the characteristic peaks, which validates the composition of α-Fe2O3@CDs was formed successfully. The FTIR result of BiOCl exhibits peaks at around 3672 and 1393 cm −1 which refer to OH bending vibrations. The apparent peaks at 1253 and 1053 cm−1 belong to the characteristic bands of BiCl and the intense peak at 535 cm −1 corresponds BiO stretching mode, indicating the valent structure was formed efficiently [74,75] .

To determine the pore chemistry of the samples N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were utilized (Fig. 2.c-d). All of the adsorption-desorption isotherms show Type III category H3 hysteresis loop indicating the presence of mesoporous structure. The specific surface areas (SSA) and total pore volume (TPV) of α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3@CDs, and BiOCl are 5.66m2/g; 0.031 cm3/g, 18.04 m2/g; 0.040 cm3/g and 6.93 m2/g; 0.044cm3/g, respectively. The SSA of α-Fe2O3 was enhanced three times with the addition of CDs.

Raman spectra of the lattice vibrations regions of the samples are demonstrated in Fig. 3 .a-d. In Fig. 3.a the peaks determined at around 217 cm−1,298 cm−1,412 cm−1,494 cm−1, and 613 cm −1 belong to the idiosyncratic Raman bands of α-Fe2O3 which can be ascribed to the A1g, E1g, E1g, A1g, and E1g Raman modes, respectively [76,77]. As shown in Fig. 3.b the corresponding Raman peaks of CDs are seen apparently as well as all of the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3.The depicted two broad peaks at around 1560 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 that could be ascribed to the D (sp2 carbon) bands, and G (sp3 carbon) respectively. The vibrations of carbon atoms with tumbling bands in the terminating plane of disordered glassy carbon or graphite correspond to the D band. The G band is identical to graphite's mode E2g and corresponds to the vibration of sp2‑carbon in the hexagonal lattice of 2D carbon (Fig. 3.c) [78]. Raman spectra of BiOCl is illustrated in Fig. 3.d. The broad band at 145 cm−1 is ascribed to the A1g internal BiCl stretching mode. The band of 198 cm−1 is relevant the Eg internal BiCl stretching mode. Additionally, the band depicted at 395 cm−1 refers to the Eg and B1g modes caused by the motion of oxygen atoms [79].

Fig. 4 presents the surface morphology of the samples revealed by FESEM and STEM. In Fig. 4.a-b FESEM and STEM images of α-Fe2O3 nanowires can be seen. Fig. 4.c-d shows the morphology of samples doped with CDs. The small sized CDs were not apparently observable in the images. Although the structure preserves the morphology, it can be inferred that the functional groups of CDs affected the homogeneity of the nanowires. Agglomerated and interwoven particles also validate the accomplished combination of the materials. Additionally, from FESEM images of BiOCl, ultra-layered flower-like BiOCl morphology is also observed (Fig. 4.e-f). Fig. 4.g presents individual elemental mapping images of Fe, O, C, N, Bi, Cl, and EDX spectra. EDX results of α-Fe2O3@CDs confirms the existence of C (11.32 %), N (3.43 %) elements in the composite as implied by XRD and FTIR and Raman analyzes.

The conspectus of the electrochemical characterization consequences is shown in Fig. S4† and Fig. 5a-c. In Fig. S4† individual CV curves with different scan rates (20–50-100 mV s−1) of (a-c): α-Fe2O3, (d-f), α-Fe2O3@CDs in a three-electrode system in 2 M KOH and, 2 M NaOH, besides a comparison of the two different electrolytes within the potential range of −0.1-0.65 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) are demonstrated, respectively. The maximum specific capacitances were obtained in 2 M KOH electrolytes for iron-based materials compared to 2 M NaOH. Thanks to the smaller hydrated radius of the K+ ions (3.31 Å), in comparison to Li+ (3.82 Å) and Na+ (3.58 Å) ions, the conductivity of K+ (73 cm2/Ω mol) at 25 °C is higher than Li+ (38 cm2/Ω mol) and Na+(50 cm2/Ω mol) ions which results in higher ionic mobility yet better collaboration with the electrode [80]. Moreover, K+ ions attain a low charge density due to weak solvation interactions with water molecules favoring easier polarization during the de-solvation processes. This characteristic aids K+ ions in reaching the electrode during the redox reactions, implying that the cations played a significant role in the electrochemical reaction processes [81–83] .Although, NaOH is a stronger base (pH of 2 M NaOH: ~13.9 experimentally) than KOH (pH of 2 M KOH: ~13.5 experimentally), solutions of KOH are about 5 % more conductive than NaOH solutions at the same molar concentration. Because of the distinctness in molecular weights, a particular mass of KOH will have approximately 30 % lesser analogs of reactant than the equal mass of NaOH. As a consequence, KOH utilization induced an increase in the electrochemical performance of the electrodes as compared to NaOH usage. When the same molarity of NaOH and KOH are analogized, it can be deduced that pH is not a momentous parameter for this study. Nevertheless, the ion concentration rises with the increase of molarity, this may have an unfavorable effect on the charge transport resistance (Rct), and the strong base structure may adversely affect the nickel foam, which has been pre-treated with strong acid (HCl), and may damage the electrode.

The discernible redox peaks connotating the faradaic feature of the materials are observed from all of the CV curves [84–86] .With the increment of the scan rates, the polarization effect occurred more obviously which arouses the anodic and cathodic peaks to shift towards the larger potential range and an expansion of the area under the curves. Notwithstanding the increment of the current density with the scan rate, all of the materials had conspicuous redox peaks, validating the efficacious charge/discharge reversibility of the materials [87]. The specific capacitance (Sc) measurement of the electrodes with discrete scan rates was done according to the Eq. (1).

In Fig. S4† (a-b) individual CV curves of α-Fe2O3 in 2 M KOH and 2 M NaOH are shown. It can be syllogized that the oxidation and reduction peaks of α-Fe2O3 are more distinct and the area under the CV curve is wider (Fig. S4†.c) in 2 M KOH. Although, the oxidation peak of α-Fe2O3 appeared at a wider potential range in NaOH. The recorded highest Sc of α-Fe2O3 was 177 g −1;139 F g −1 in 2 M KOH; 2 M NaOH at a scan rate of 5 mV s −1, respectively. The Sc changes with discrete scan rates are shown in Fig. S6†.a. In Fig. 5.a-b) the CV measurements of the α-Fe2O3@CDs composite are shown. When the CV curves of the material have an akin form in both electrolytes, the measurement area was higher in 2 M KOH as seen apparently in Fig. 5.a. The maximum Scs were reckoned as 2453 F g −1 and 1982 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s −1 in 2 M KOH and 2 M NaOH, respectively. The Scs variation with the scan rates is graphed in Fig. 5.b. As seen obviously, with the addition of CDs there is a remarkable increase in Scs of α-Fe2O3.

The CV curves of BiOCl representative of the distinctive CV shapes of Bi-based materials with two obvious oxidation peaks and one broad reduction peak in 2 M KOH were also can be seen in Fig. 5.c [45,46,74,88] .With a similar approach, the polarization effect caused the shifts in potential ranges for both anodic and cathodic peaks and the surrounded area by CV curve was increased with increment scan rates [89]. The highest Sc of the material was 344 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 (Fig. 5.d).

Charge-discharge mechanism and cyclic stability after a long time of the electrodes at different current densities (1 A g−1- 40 A g−1) were investigated by applying GCD measurements. The consonant potential window for iron-based materials was - 0.1 to 0.40 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 2 M KOH, and 2 M NaOH aqueous electrolytes, when the potential range of BiOCl was identic with the working potential of CV curves; −1.2-0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The occurred further interplay between the electrode and electroactive surface of OH− ions at low currents induces boosted Scs values. Nevertheless, the transpired diffusion effect at high current densities leads to a reduction in specific capacitance [90–92]. İdiomatical GCD curves (a-d) -(b-e) and the comparison curves (c-f) in the different electrolytes of pristine α-Fe2O3 are shown in Fig. S5†.a. The Sc changes with discrete current densities are shown in Fig. S6†.b. The highest Scs, calculated via Eq. (2), for all electrodes are obtained in 2 M KOH electrolyte at a current density of 1 A g−1. The charge-discharge times of α-Fe2O3@CDs are exceedingly longer than α-Fe2O3 indicates 0D CDs with 25 nm size and multifunctional N-including functional groups have pivotal effect on the electrochemical features. Individual GCD curves in 2 M KOH, and 2 M NaOH aqueous electrolytes at the current density of 1 A g−1 and Scs results of α-Fe2O3@CDs at the different current densities are illustrated in Fig. 6a-b. The composite reached the highest Sc of 3686 Fg−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 in 2 M KOH, when the maximum Sc was 2060 F g−1 in 2 M NaOH at the same current density. Additionally, GCD measurement results of BİOCl with the potential window of −1.2- 0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) are demonstrated in Fig. 6.c-d. A distinct leap observed around - 0.8 Volt can be attributed to the characteristic peak seen on bismuth-based materials [93–96]. When the BİOCl electrode reached the highest Sc of 380 Fg−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, the Sc was 200 F g−1 at a current density of 20 A g−1(Fig. 6.d).

Further to designate the long-term cyclic stability of the electrodes long-term chronopotentiometry analysis was implemented at a current density of 20 Ag −1 up to 5000 cycles for iron-based materials, and 10,000 cycles for BiOCl. The retention of α-Fe2O3 was 75 %; 69 % in 2 M KOH and 2 M NaOH electrolytes, the retention of α-Fe2O3@CDs was 85 %; 84 % (2 M KOH; 2 M NaOH) as shown in Fig. 6.e indicating CDs addition improve the cyclic stability of α-Fe2O3. Additionally, the retention of BiOCl was % 85 after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 6.f).

Analyzation of the kinetics of ion and charge transmission behavior of the electrodes was done by implementing the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at the high and low-frequency range, 0.01 to 100 kHz, with an amplitude of 5 mV. When the high-frequency region is causative to the charge transmission, the low-frequency region designates the resistance of ion diffusion [97]. İon diffusion between the electro-active area of the electrode and electrolyte interfaces engenders an additional line denominated the Warburg line which generates a 45°angle among that vertical line and the x-axis. In Fig. S7† the distinctive Nyquist plots of α-Fe2O3 are demonstrated. Additionally, EIS graphics of α-Fe2O3@CDs and BİOCl are demonstrated in Fig. 7 .a-b.The recorded charge transport resistance (Rct) and the equivalent series resistance (Rs) in different electrolytes are tabularized in Table S3†. It can be syllogized that among iron-based materials, α-Fe2O3 has the lowest Rct and Rs which increased with the addition of CDs indicating N inclusive functional groups aroused extra resists.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of the asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: To check the realistic application performance of the electrodes, an ASC device was congregated by forming α-Fe2O3@CDs as cathode and, BiOCl as anode with a glass microfibre filter separator primary soaked for 2 h in 2 M KOH electrolyte. Mass balance of cathode and anode electrodes was reckoned ~3 according to Eq. (3).

Electrochemical productivities of the ASC device are exhibited in Fig. 8 . When Fig. 8.a shows the schematic representation of the device, distinctive CV curves of the anode (BiOCl), cathode (α-Fe2O3@CDs) at a scan rate of 50 mV s −1 are shown in Fig. 8.b. To determine the adequate potential range various potential window between 0 and 1.2 V to 0–1.8 V was checked (Fig. 8.c) The appropriate potential window was chosen as 0–1.8 V since electrolysis of water restricts to operate of wider potentials. In Fig. 8.d. CV curves of the ASC device in the cell voltage limit of 1.8 V at the different scan rates are demonstrated. The distinctly depicted reduction and oxidation peaks affirm the pseudocapacitive nature of the device since it compounds many elements in the structure.

The GCD features of the device were investigated at different current densities and graphed in Fig. 8.e. Though there was not an apparent time difference between the charge-discharge process, the un-linear conformation of the GCD curves indicates that the composite material shows high pseudocapacitive nature thanks to the multi-elemental structure. When the ASC device reached the highest specific capacitance of 251 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, the recorded minimum specific capacitance was 128 F g−1 at a current density of 40 Ag−1. The other variations are shown in Fig. 8.f. The performance of the device was checked during 10,000 continuous long-term GCD. The recorded 83 % degradation confirms the device has adequate cyclic stability (Fig. 9 .a). The Nyquist plot of the ASC was drawn to investigate the charging kinetics of the device (Fig. 9.b), and the obtained Rs and Rct values were 0,63 Ω,17 m Ω, respectively.

To specify the comparison between the energy and power densities of the ACS the Ragone plot was drawn (Fig. 9.c). For the assembled device when the highest energy density was recorded as 28 Wh kg −1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, the obtained maximum power density was 14,823 W kg−1 at a current density of 40 A g−1. The details are in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of the zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Combination of the battery type structured zinc, and high pseudocapacitive α-Fe2O3@CDs ended up highly efficient ZHSC which was assembled by the direct composition of Zn foil as both anode and current collector when α-Fe2O3@CDs as cathode, 2 M KOH +0.05 M ZnSO₄ as the electrolyte, and a glassy fiber filter paper as the separator at the potential range of 0–7- 2.2 V. Based on the superior properties of KOH,as mentioned in the previous section, it was approved to use 2 M KOH besides a zinc-based electrolyte (0.05 M ZnSO₄) to boost the electrochemical performance of ZHSC device.

In the ZHSC device, as indicated in Fig. 10a, the K+ ion disinsertion/insertion procedure transpires at the cathode when the Zn conglomeration/disintegration occurs at the anode during the charge-discharge step. Faraday's equation for the capacity (i.e., the quantity of charge) at the cathode side of ZHSC can be indicated as Q = n.F, where n is the number of electrons (e), and F is the Faraday constant [25,98].

The electrochemical performance of the ZHSC device is illustrated in Fig. 10. The schematic representation of ZHSC device is shown in Fig. 10.a when Fig. 10.b presents CV curves of α-Fe2O3@CDs //2 M KOH +0.05 M ZnSO₄ // Zn foil ZHSC device at various scan rates. Well-apparent redox peaks indicate the system shows a high pseudocapacitive effect even at high scan rates. When the voltage range of ZHSC is 0.2–1.8 Volt via the addition of KOH we ably enhanced the voltage up to 2.2 Volt. However, the optimized lowest potential according to reduction peaks was 0.7 Volt.

According to CV curves, the peak current (i) and scan rate (v) manage to the charge storage mechanism of electrodes as follows.
(13)
i = a
v b
where i is the current (A), v is the scan rate (mV s−1), and a and b are constants. Constant b value is calculated by fitting linear line (log(i) vs log (v)). The situation b-value equals to 1, the charge storage is managed by capacitive process, while b-value equals to 0.5 expressing charge storage is controlled by diffusion [14] .As illustrated in Fig. 10.c, the b-values are 0.60–0.50 for anodic and cathodic reactions showing that charge storage behavior is influenced by both capacitive and diffusion processes.

To further analyze the charge-discharge mechanism of the ZHSC device GCD measurement was performed (Fig. 10.d.). The unsymmetrical charge-discharge curves manifest the hybrid structure of the ZHSC device. Eq. (2) was used to calculate the Scs of the ZHSC device at different current densities. When the ZHSC reached the maximum SCs of 449 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, the minimum SCs were recorded as 178 F g−1 at a current density of 40 A g−1 (Fig. S8†.a). The graphed Nyquist plot (Fig. 10.e) also remarks that the device has a low equivalent series resistance of (Rs) 1.65 Ω although, the charge transport resistance (Rct) of 2.48 Ω was much higher than pristine materials. To further check the stability of the device 15,000 continuous GCD measurements at the current density of 20 A g−1 were applied and the Sc degradation was reported after 15,000 cycles as 22 % (Fig. S8†.b). As seen from the schematic illustration in Fig. 10.f the Ragon plot substantiates the energy and power densities of the ZHSC in the region of the ionic supercapacitor. The energy (E, Wh kg−1), and the power (P, W kg−1) densities of the ZHSC device were tabularized in Table 3 , which were measured from the Eqs. (5) and (6). The maximum energy density was recorded as 140 Wh kg−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1 (~5 x ASC;28 Wh kg−1), and the highest power density was 30 kW kg−1 at the current density of 40 A g−1 (~2 x ASC; 14 kW kg−1).

The acquired outcomes are consistent /superior to those previously reported for BiOCl and α-Fe2O3 based SSC and ASC devices, which are tabularized in Table 4 with SC (device), electrolyte, potential range (V), ED (Wh kg−1), PD (W kg−1), and cyclic stability.

To conclude, herein we initially investigated the nano-size effect on the electrochemical performance of α-Fe2O3 by unitarily the pristine material with OD carbon dots (CDs). Two different electrolytes (2 M KOH: NaOH) were utilized to optimize the productivity . Thereafter, the best-yielded α-Fe2O3@CDs (2453 F g −1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1; 3686 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g −1 in 2 M KOH electrolyte) electrode was formed as the cathode material to both an ASC and ZHSC with BiOCl, Zn foil as the anodes. The congregated α-Fe2O3@CDs // 2 M KOH // BiOCl ASC device reached a high specific capacitance of 251 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with a wide voltage of 1.8 V. the maximum energy and power densities were 28 Wh kg−1 and 14.8 kW kg−1, respectively. Additionally, the produced ZHSC achieved the maximum specific capacitance of 449 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with 22 % degradation after 15.000 cycles. Moreover, the highest energy and power densities were 140 Wh kg−1 (~5 x ASC), and 30 kW kg−1,(~2 x ASC), respectively. With these impressive features α-Fe2O3@CDs hybrid material can be thinkable as a promising cathode material for both ASC and ZHSC.
